mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Albania on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, on the south-east coast of the Balkan Peninsula. Albania faces the Adriatic and Ionian Seas, with Italy just across the water 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Albania?
mapQuestions.answer: Tirana, in the centre. About 600,000 people. Famous for its brightly painted communist-era apartment blocks (a former mayor decided to brighten up the city by ordering them all painted in bold colours).

mapQuestions.question: What language does Albania speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Albanian (shqip), a unique Indo-European language that doesn't belong to any of the big language families around it (Slavic, Greek, Romance). One of the oldest languages in Europe.

mapQuestions.question: Why are there 170,000 concrete bunkers in Albania?
mapQuestions.answer: Albania spent decades (1944 to 1991) as an isolated communist state under leader Enver Hoxha, who was paranoid about invasion. He built around 170,000 small concrete bunkers across the country. Most are still there as quirky reminders.

mapQuestions.question: How long was Albania isolated?
mapQuestions.answer: 46 years (1944 to 1991). For most of the Cold War, Albania was the most isolated country in Europe, with hardly any tourism, religion banned, and travel abroad almost impossible.

foods.name: Byrek
foods.description: A flaky pastry pie filled with cheese, spinach, meat or pumpkin. Similar to Croatia's burek (but Albanians will tell you theirs is much older and better). The everyday Albanian snack.

foods.name: Fërgesë
foods.description: A baked dish of peppers, tomato, garlic and salty white cheese, served bubbling in a clay dish with fresh bread. The signature Tirana dish.

foods.name: Tavë Kosi
foods.description: Lamb (or chicken) baked under a thick layer of yogurt and rice. Albania's national dish, originally from Elbasan.

foods.name: Honey + walnuts
foods.description: Wild mountain honey poured over walnuts (often with yogurt). A classic Albanian breakfast. Albanian honey is very highly prized.

foods.name: Speca të mbushura
foods.description: Bell peppers stuffed with rice, mince and herbs, baked in tomato sauce. A common family Sunday lunch.

foods.name: Trilece
foods.description: A sweet sponge cake soaked in three milks (whole, condensed, evaporated) topped with caramel sauce. Originally Latin American but adopted into Balkan dessert culture.

scavengerItems.label: Skanderbeg Square in Tirana with the equestrian statue of Skanderbeg
A brightly painted communist apartment block (the Tirana mayor's art project)
A medieval Ottoman-era castle (Krujë, Berat, Gjirokastër)
A "thousand windows" Ottoman town (Berat, UNESCO World Heritage)
A working bunker (some are now small museums, cafés or wineries)
A clear-water Ionian beach on the "Albanian Riviera"
The Albanian Alps (the "Accursed Mountains") in the north
An Orthodox church and an Ottoman mosque side by side (Albania is famous for religious tolerance)
The red flag with the black double-headed eagle (the national symbol)
Old men playing dominoes at outdoor tables in a town square
Local wild mountain honey for sale at a roadside stand
A 1970s Mercedes still running (Mercedes are a strange national passion in Albania)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Bright, fast-changing, full of street art, cafés and brightly painted blocks. Skanderbeg Square is the heart.
The "town of a thousand windows", an Ottoman-era hill town of white houses with stacked windows climbing up a steep slope. UNESCO World Heritage.
A stone-built UNESCO Ottoman city in the south, with a giant hilltop castle and traditional stone-slate houses cascading down the hillside.
The southern Ionian coast: clear turquoise water, white-pebble beaches, small fishing villages (Himara, Dhërmi, Ksamil), still much cheaper than Greece or Croatia.
The "Accursed Mountains" in the far north. Spectacular hiking between Theth and Valbona villages, traditional stone "kullë" tower houses, untouched alpine scenery.
An archaeological site in the south with ruins from Greek, Roman, Byzantine, Venetian and Ottoman periods, all on top of each other. UNESCO World Heritage.

senses.smell: Wood smoke from village ovens, mountain herbs (sage, oregano, thyme) on a hillside, espresso at every café, the salty Ionian breeze, freshly baked byrek pastry…
senses.hear: "Faleminderit!" and "Mirëdita!", iso-polyphonic singing in the south (a UNESCO-protected vocal tradition), Mercedes diesel engines, mountain rivers in the north, espresso cups clinking…
senses.taste: Salty tangy fërgesë cheese-and-pepper, fresh mountain yogurt, sharp lemon over grilled fish, sweet mountain honey, strong Albanian espresso…
senses.feel: The polish of an Ottoman cobblestone, soft Berat-window-frame wood, hot Ionian beach pebbles, the cool of an Albanian Alps valley in summer, the warm of someone's grandmother hugging you on arrival…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are there bunkers everywhere?
stories.body: During the Cold War, Albania's communist leader Enver Hoxha was paranoid about invasion (from Yugoslavia, NATO, the USSR, China, basically everyone). He ordered around 170,000 small concrete bunkers built across the country, in fields, on beaches, in mountain passes, in schoolyards. The bunkers were never used: no invasion ever came. After communism ended in 1991, the bunkers became cultural oddities. Some are still in their original spots. A few have been turned into mini-museums ("Bunk'Art" in Tirana), small cafés, wineries, hostel rooms, even art installations. They are now a unique part of Albania's identity.

stories.question: Why are the apartment blocks so colourful?
stories.body: When Edi Rama (a former painter) became mayor of Tirana in 2000, the city was grey, dusty, and recovering from decades of communist rule. He decided to paint the grim concrete apartment blocks in bright bold colours: orange, pink, lime green, electric blue, geometric patterns. The change was dramatic. People stopped littering and started taking care of the city more. Tirana's painted blocks became famous around the world as a low-cost example of how public art can change a city's mood. Rama is now Albania's prime minister. You can still see his paint job everywhere in Tirana.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A city was changed just by painting its buildings in bright colours. What small visual change could improve your home town?

stories.question: Why has the Albanian coast taken so long to be discovered?
stories.body: Albania has some of the most beautiful Adriatic and Ionian beaches in Europe, with clear blue water, white pebbles, and very few hotels. But for most of the 20th century, Albania was sealed off from the world: under communism (1944 to 1991), nearly no foreign tourists could visit. When Albania reopened, the country was poor, infrastructure was broken, and most travellers went to better-known beaches in Greece or Croatia instead. Only in the last 10 years has the Albanian Riviera (Saranda, Ksamil, Dhërmi, Himara) started becoming a serious European beach destination. Prices are still much lower than nearby countries. Catch it now.

stories.question: Why is Berat called "the city of 1,000 windows"?
stories.body: The historic part of Berat is built on a steep hillside, with hundreds of white-walled Ottoman houses stacked up the slope, almost on top of each other. Each house has rows of small wooden windows facing the river. Seen from across the river at sunset, the whole hillside seems to glow with thousands of windows reflecting the light. Berat has been continuously lived in for over 2,400 years. UNESCO put the historic centres of Berat and Gjirokastër on the World Heritage list in 2008 for their preserved Ottoman architecture.

stories.question: Why is Albanian religion so tolerant?
stories.body: Albania has Muslims (about 58%), Orthodox Christians (about 7%), Catholic Christians (about 10%), and a long tradition of small Bektashi (Sufi mystical) communities, all living side by side. During the Ottoman centuries, communities mixed in unusual ways. There is an Albanian saying: "The religion of Albanians is Albanianism." During World War II, Albania (then briefly free from invasion) became one of the only countries in Europe where the Jewish population actually GREW, because Albanians of all religions hid Jewish refugees in their homes, refused to hand over names, and protected them. Religious tolerance is taught proudly as a national value.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Albania so far?
Capital, mountain, beach, ruins, which has felt the most "Albania"?
Have you seen a bunker? What was it like?
How does the Albanian coast compare to other European coasts you have been to?
Albania was almost closed off from the world for 46 years and then opened completely. How does that history feel as you walk around?

wordSearch.hint: Albanian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: The national bird, on the flag and the country's self-name ("Shqipëria" means "Land of Eagles"). Lives in the Albanian Alps.

animals.name: Mountain goat
animals.description: In the rugged northern Accursed Mountains and along the Albanian-Macedonian border.

animals.name: Donkey
animals.description: Working donkeys still common in rural mountain villages, carrying goods up steep paths where cars cannot go.

animals.name: Mediterranean tortoise
animals.description: Sometimes spotted in the dry limestone hills of southern Albania near Saranda and Butrint.

animals.name: Red fox
animals.description: Common across Albania, especially in the foothills around villages.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Returning slowly to the Albanian Alps. Population is very small.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: A small but genuine wolf population in the eastern mountains.
